_doc: Operational definition of 'known to be not toxic' for comp-014 species inclusion. Replaces the curated-candidate-list approach. Phase 2 pulls fungal-compound records species-agnostically from LOTUS / NPAtlas / KNApSAcK / NPASS / TCMSP, then applies this filter to the unique species list that emerges. Inclusion-OR (any positive match → include); Exclusion-OR (any positive match → drop, regardless of inclusion-list status — exclusion takes precedence).
_principle: Brian framing 2026-05-06: 'we don't want to get every fungus, but we don't want to get toxic ones. So can we get the ones that are known to be not toxic?' Operationalized via authoritative safety lists rather than Claude's judgment.

FDA_GRAS_Notice_Inventory.scope: FDA Generally Recognized As Safe notices for food use. Explicit positive safety review.
FDA_GRAS_Notice_Inventory.use: Any fungal species with a GRAS Notice (any GRN number) is automatically included.

EFSA_QPS_list.scope: EFSA Qualified Presumption of Safety — biennially-updated list of food-grade microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, filamentous fungi). Most recent update typically late in calendar year.
EFSA_QPS_list.use: Any fungal species on the QPS list (and the species-complex it represents) is included.

Codex_Alimentarius_food_use.scope: FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius — international food safety standards including approved fungi for food and food-additive production.
Codex_Alimentarius_food_use.use: Any fungus with documented Codex food-use precedent included.

sources: Chinese Pharmacopoeia (中华人民共和国药典) — current edition + historical materia medica references via TCMID/TCMSP/HIT
Japanese Pharmacopoeia (日本薬局方) — current edition + Kampo materia medica
Korean Pharmacopoeia (대한민국약전) — current edition + traditional Korean medicine references
United States Pharmacopoeia (USP) — herbal section + dietary supplement monographs
Traditional_pharmacopoeia.scope: Listing in any current pharmacopoeia (esp. herbal/traditional sections) implies long-standing human use without acute toxicity flag.
Traditional_pharmacopoeia.use: Cross-reference Phase 2 species list against pharmacopoeia herbal/traditional sections.

sources: ClinicalTrials.gov (search filter: intervention contains species name)
WHO ICTRP (International Clinical Trials Registry Platform)
PubMed published RCTs and Phase I-IV trials
Published_human_clinical_trials.scope: Any fungal species that has been administered to humans in a registered clinical trial implies a regulatory safety review preceded the trial.
Published_human_clinical_trials.use: Inclusion if ≥1 trial registry entry exists, regardless of trial outcome.
examples: Trametes versicolor PSK (FDA-approved adjuvant in Japan)
Ganoderma lucidum (numerous trials including FDA-IND-cleared studies)

LOTUS_NPAtlas_records_without_mycotoxin_.scope: Compounds appearing in LOTUS or NPAtlas with a fungal source-organism annotation, where neither the compound nor the source species appears in the mycotoxin reference DB.
LOTUS_NPAtlas_records_without_mycotoxin_.use: Default inclusion for less-studied species — environmental isolate or research-only fungus with characterized chemistry but no documented human exposure.
LOTUS_NPAtlas_records_without_mycotoxin_.flag: Mark as 'safety_review_pending' so Phase 4-5 re-checks before downstream recommendation.

WHO_Fungal_Priority_Pathogens_2022.scope: WHO 2022 priority pathogens list — 19 fungal species causing serious human invasive disease.
WHO_Fungal_Priority_Pathogens_2022.use: Hard exclusion — any species on this list is dropped regardless of compound bioactivity. Note: this excludes most Fusarium spp. and Aspergillus fumigatus, but A. oryzae / A. niger / A. sojae remain included via GRAS/QPS — Aspergillus genus does not exclude as a whole.

sources: JECFA monographs (FAO/WHO Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives)
Mycotoxin_producers.scope: Documented producers of acutely toxic compounds at human-exposure-relevant doses.
exclude_species_dominated_by_toxin_profi: Claviceps purpurea (ergot alkaloids — historical interest only; LSD origin)
Mycotoxin_producers.use: Hard exclusion. If a compound from one of these species has chokepoint relevance, it gets manual-review status — the chemistry may be valuable but the producing organism cannot be the production chassis.

Schedule_I_II_controlled_substance_produ.scope: DEA Schedule I/II controlled-substance-producing fungi. Not actionable for Open Enzyme commercial path.
Schedule_I_II_controlled_substance_produ.use: Hard exclusion for breadth pass. Research interest exists separately; defer to clinical trial channel.

Plant_pathogens_dominated_by_toxin_profi.scope: Agricultural plant pathogens whose human-relevant compound profile is dominated by mycotoxins or whose ecological role is causing crop disease.
examples: Magnaporthe oryzae (rice blast — no documented medicinal chemistry)
Phytophthora spp. (oomycete, not true fungi but often grouped)
Plant_pathogens_dominated_by_toxin_profi.use: Soft exclusion — if a compound from one of these species shows chokepoint relevance via Phase 4 intersection, manual review.

grey_zone_protocol.definition: Species appearing in NEITHER inclusion list NOR exclusion list. Most likely environmental isolates with no documented human exposure data — neither evidence-of-safety nor evidence-of-harm.
grey_zone_protocol.Phase_4_5_treatment: If chokepoint intersection promotes a grey-zone species as a candidate, Phase 5 does explicit human-exposure-data lookup before any downstream recommendation. Look for: documented natural occurrence in foodstuffs, documented allergenic reactions, documented case reports of clinical infection.

expected_yield.rough_estimate: 500-2000 unique species after filtering across LOTUS + NPAtlas + KNApSAcK + NPASS + TCMSP. Phase 2a (LOTUS only) likely surfaces ~500-1000 fungal species. Phase 2b (NPAtlas) is microbial-NP focused with strong fungal coverage, likely ~300-500 distinct species after dedup with LOTUS. KNApSAcK adds the East-Asian-traditional-medicine corpus.
expected_yield.anchor_species_check: phase-5-anchor-species.json contains 18 well-studied medicinal fungi. The breadth pass should surface all 18 — if any are missing from the Phase 2 output, that indicates a pipeline bug (filter too aggressive, or DB pull missed records). Sanity-check fail triggers re-run, not soft-fail-with-warning.
